title: Ending USMCA could fuel higher US consumer prices
gist: PIIE modeling shows that replacing USMCA with a 15% tariff on Canadian and Mexican goods would raise the US PCE price index by about 0.27% overall and roughly 2.7% for autos specifically, with the true impact likely understated because $310 billion of intermediate-goods imports aren't captured in the consumer-price calculation.

themes.theme: USMCA uncertainty and the tariff threat

insights.claim: The US government's recent refusal to renew USMCA leaves the trade pact technically in effect but its future unclear, since it will now be subject to annual review for a decade unless the parties renew or end it.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Creates a rolling, decade-long overhang of policy uncertainty for any business with Canada/Mexico supply-chain exposure, rather than a one-time resolved event.

insights.claim: President Trump has mentioned the possibility of terminating USMCA entirely and separately has talked about imposing a global tariff of 15%, which together define the scenario this analysis models.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Sets up a concrete, plausible policy scenario (15% tariff replacing USMCA preferences) that investors should treat as a real tail risk, not a remote hypothetical.

insights.claim: A permanent, comprehensive 15% US tariff would almost certainly require congressional assent, which is unlikely if the November 2026 elections give Democrats control of the House of Representatives.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Ties the probability of this tariff scenario materializing directly to the outcome of the 2026 midterms, giving investors a concrete political catalyst to watch.

themes.theme: Structural US dependence on Canada and Mexico

insights.claim: Canada and Mexico supplied 26.9% of all US goods imports in 2025, with majority shares in specific categories including crude oil (67.1%), natural gas (94.5%), motor vehicles for goods transport (95.7%), and vehicle parts (55.6%).
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Identifies exactly which product categories (energy, autos) carry the highest near-term supply-chain and price risk if USMCA preferences end.
insights.locator: table: Canada and Mexico dominate US imports in major consumer goods categories

insights.claim: These import categories, particularly oil, motor vehicles, and auto parts, would be difficult to quickly source from other countries given how concentrated supply currently is in Canada and Mexico.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Limits near-term substitution options, meaning tariff costs on these categories would likely pass through to consumers rather than be avoided via resourcing.

insights.claim: 88.2% of Mexican and Canadian consumer imports were USMCA-compliant as of December 2025, up sharply from 40% in early 2025, as companies adjusted supply chains to meet the pact's rules of origin.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Shows firms made significant, costly compliance investments to qualify for USMCA preferences over the past year — investments that would be stranded if the pact ends, amplifying disruption.

insights.claim: Combining the shares of consumer imports from Canada/Mexico (24.8%), the USMCA-compliant share of those imports (88.2%), a 15% tariff, a roughly 92% tariff pass-through rate to consumers, and the 9% share of PCE traceable to imports, replacing USMCA with a 15% tariff would raise the US PCE price index by about 0.27%.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Gives a single, headline-usable inflation impact figure (0.27% on PCE) that can be directly compared against Fed inflation targets and other tariff-scenario estimates.

insights.claim: The same 0.27% price-impact estimate is independently cross-validated using OECD value-added-in-trade data, which shows 1.1 percentage points of US consumption value-added from Canada and 0.7 percentage points from Mexico, multiplied by the 15% tariff.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Two independent methodologies converging on the same 0.27% figure strengthens confidence in the estimate as a reasonable base case for tariff-driven inflation impact.

insights.claim: The auto sector alone would see roughly a 2.7% consumer price increase under a 15% tariff scenario, reflecting Canada and Mexico's outsized 18.07% combined value-added share in US auto consumption.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Autos would face a price shock roughly 10x the economy-wide average, making the sector the clearest concentrated risk for consumers and automakers if USMCA ends.

insights.claim: $310 billion in industrial intermediate goods were imported from Canada and Mexico in 2025 — 22% of all US industrial intermediate imports and 34% of all US imports from the two partners — meaning the consumer-price estimates likely understate the true impact since many intermediate goods eventually reach consumers as finished products not captured in the direct calculation.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: The author explicitly flags the headline 0.27% figure as a likely underestimate, meaning the real consumer price risk from ending USMCA could be materially higher than the base-case number.

insights.claim: Canada and Mexico could impose retaliatory tariffs in response to a US move to end USMCA, which would additionally harm US exporters beyond the direct consumer-price effects modeled in this analysis.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: The modeled 0.27%/2.7% price impacts are a floor, not a ceiling — retaliation risk adds an unquantified additional drag on US exporters.

insights.claim: Coming after an unwelcome rise in consumer prices this year driven by an oil price spike, a further price jolt from 15% tariffs on Mexican and Canadian goods could prove politically unpopular with American voters.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer
insights.so_what: Links the economic scenario to political risk ahead of the November 2026 midterms, suggesting the tariff threat itself may carry electoral consequences that could deter full implementation.
